sentences.text: Yes.
We made everything ourselves.
Because there was no grocery that we could go in and buy.
So everything had to be handmade.
So we made our potato starch from the potatoes.
We put away-- after the-- when we gathered together from the fields, the potatoes and carrots and everything, we put it away in the ground for Pesach already.
So we dug out a hole.
And we put some straw.
And then we put the potatoes and carrots and everything, the turnips, the [NON-ENGLISH] for the soup.
And before Pesach, we would take it out.
So it was fresh like-- like we just picked it out from the fields.
So we did everything.
We had our own geese.
So we made the [NON-ENGLISH] ourselves.
Through the Hanukkah time we would eat the geese.
And the [NON-ENGLISH], we would put away the fat for the-- for Pesach.
So we did everything-- the-- ourselves.
We didn't use too many things-- potatoes, some meat, and turnips for Pesach.
Because it was very observed.
And we had, of course, dishes separate.
And my father had the-- the cup for the wine, and the plate, Seder plate, from his grandfather.
So nobody was allowed to touch that.
Because he would put it away, he would take it out, he would wash it.
Because it was very precious to him.
And I'm sorry that we don't have anything from home to-- to take care on, like my father took care on his precious things.
Well, he said it in Hebrew.
And then he translated it in Yiddish for us.
Each paragraph was translated in Yiddish.
And it was done, sometimes, 2 o'clock in the morning.
We were, the first night, very tired.
But he made sure that we hear every word of it, and we hear everything.
And it was conducted to 100%.
My brothers.
My brothers asked the questions.
Not the girls.
The girls didn't participate [INAUDIBLE].
But he let us-- he let us sing with him the [NON-ENGLISH].
If Saturday, we were sitting at the table, he-- he would let us.
He says, the very religious people wouldn't allow it.
But he will allow us, the girls, to help along to sing with the boys and with him, the [NON-ENGLISH]
Very good.
Excellent.
We, somehow, I think, because we didn't have a mother, I always felt for my brothers, so sorry that they don't have a mother.
And we helped one another all the time.
Because my father always said, if you-- we're going to stick together, we're going to make it.
And he always emphasizes for this that we have to help one another.
And that's exactly what it was.
We always helped one another.
If-- if my-- if I would go to the well to bring water, my brother would grab the pail from me.
I says, I'm stronger.
So he says, no, you do other things that I can't do.
I'll bring the water, I'll fetch the water.
And my sister, the same thing with my sisters.
My sister-- she-- she used to come into my-- I took care on the house, already, when I was a very young age.
And she learned how to sew, to be a seamstress.
She would sneak out from there, come and help me a little bit with the house-- housework.
And we always helped, till this day.
There is nothing I wouldn't do for my sisters, and I know that my sisters would do for me.
And I know that if we wouldn't have been so close, helping one another, I would have never make it through the war.
That's for sure.
Because we got along so good, and that was my father's doing.
Because he always said, you have to help one another.
And we always got along very good.
I used to make something special for my brothers and bring it to the cheder.
Because I knew they're little, they don't have a mother to spoil them a little bit.
So I would make them a latke, or something special, and I would bring it to them, to cheder.
They loved it.
Tell me something about your brothers.
Well, my-- my oldest-- my older brother was a very, very sincere.
Always-- he was-- always, when I think of my brother, I always think of him sad.
My little brother was different.
He was more of a happy boy.
But we spoiled them.
My father didn't like the idea, that we're spoiling him.
But I always felt that he's-- he's a baby and he doesn't have a mother.
And so we spoiled him.
We made him something special.
Like, if he wanted something, and we didn't do it, he says, oh, if my mother would have lived, she would have give it to me.
That's all he had to say.
We would all start to cry.
And he got it.
And my father didn't like that idea, that we're giving in.
So he was the spoiled one.
But my brother, my older brother, I mean, he was younger than I was-- he was always very sincere and helpful.
He felt that he's the big boy around the house, that he should do the heavy work.
He was still a young kid, but my father wasn't home so he was the big boy.
Well, it was Czechoslovakia.
The Jewish people had, I think, better than the-- the peasants.
They were in business.
They wore nicer clothes.
I think they were richer than-- maybe more money.
Maybe they weren't more-- more money, but they lived-- they lived a better life than the peasants.
They had better, nicer homes, nicer clothes.
And we went to the Czech school.
And we mingled with-- with the people, like, the-- they were working in the city hall, and the-- the teachers were Czech.
We played more with those children than with the peasants' children.
No.
I mean, we lived in Czechoslovakia, but they were Ukrainians.
So when-- but they-- but we got along very good.
We used to do business with them-- with the peasants with the Jewish people.
We got along very good.
A matter of fact, I used to have, from both sides, we had non-Jewish people living.
And I played with the girls.
And then when the Czechs left in 1938, the Czech-- left Podkarpatska Rus, where we lived.
They went to Czechoslovakia.
So there was no more Czech school.
And we had to go with the Ukrainian school.
And there, I had all my friends were not Jewish.
I only had three girls in my class that were Jewish.
But they didn't live in the neighborhood.
Not far, but I-- so I played.
I walked to school with non-Jewish girls.
And we played.
And we became very friendly.
And we lived very good with them.
I think the Czechs were more nicer people than the Ukrainians.
I have to say, that they treated the Jewish people better.
They were, I think, jealous of the Jewish people.
The Ukrainian.
Because they felt that we are more-- first of all, higher education.
The Jewish people would go for higher education.
The peasants wouldn't-- didn't go.
And the Czechs were also educated people.
So I think the Czechs mingled more with the Jewish people than with the peasants.
No, no.
He didn't feel.
He always said that people are people.
You have to know where you belong, he says.
You have to know that you're Jewish, and what you could do and what you can't do.
He was never afraid that we're going to go with non-Jewish people to marry them, or something like that.
He--that was out of question.
But to play, and be their friends was just perfect for my-- in my father's eyes.
And that's how he always told us, that a person has to be looked on as a person, not what religion he is.
And you have to pick your religion.
And you have to make sure that you know how far you could go.
If you could-- we never ate in my friend's house.
I couldn't-- I know I can't eat there.
They were able to come to our house and eat.
But I went to their house to play.
My father never had to tell me that, don't eat there.
I know I can't eat there.
Or he knew that certain holidays, like--it was Christmas, that I didn't go to their house.
Because they celebrated their holidays.
So nothing to fear.
You know?
But we were very close.
My father didn't mind at all, that I had non-Jewish friends.
And he was friendly, also, with the people.
He was very friendly with them.
Well, like I said, as a child, as a child I didn't.
Because first-- but later on, when the Hungarians came, and we had to take the second language Hungarian.
So already came Hungarian teachers.
And I remember we were taking a picture.
The school was like facing the shul, or the temple.
And we were standing at the back of the shul-- school, and facing.
So the teacher says, let's not take this picture there.
Because the-- it's going to show the shul.
He says, we don't want to show any Judaism here.
And I felt so uncomfortable, the way she said it, that the shul shouldn't be in the picture.
And I thought our shul was so-- such a beautiful building, that it adds to the picture.
And she-- she didn't want it.
So but, I start feeling, also, that a lot of kids would-- became anti-Semites to us.
Like, they-- if they made a play, they wouldn't take any Jewish children in the play.
Or if they did something special in school, so the Jewish kids were excluded.
And we weren't so many.
I just had three Jewish girls, and two boys.
So we were altogether, about six children-- and-- Jewish children.
But we were excluded.
And if I have to say that-- our-- we were very smart, you know-- because our parents emphasized education.
And they-- the peasants didn't.
They wanted they should go in the fields, to work in the fields.
So they would miss a lot of school.
And we went every day to school.
My father wouldn't hear of it, we shouldn't go to school.
He wanted we should go.
He wanted we should go for-- to have our education.
No matter how bad things were, when we didn't want to go to school, my father says, no.
You have to go.
You have to learn.
You have to go on with life.
Because this is just for a while.
And then the storm will go over and you'll have to have your education.
So the Jewish people were more-- not better, but already I felt anti-Semitism, that we were excluded in school.
And these were children who used to be your friends, who began to--
These were children that used to play with us.
And I used to go out.
And I says, how come you don't want to play with me?
She says, I really want to play with you, she says.
But the children said, if I'm going to play with you, that they don't--aren't going to play with me.
I says, why?
She  says, because you're Jewish.
So I says, I played with you all the time.
She says, but it was different.
Now the Jewish people are not the same.
I says, yes, we are the same.
I says, you're not the same.
She says, no.
My friends tell me I shouldn't play with you.
So I used to come-- get very upset and come home.
And I would complain to my father about it.
And my father says, look, you have cousins, you have sisters and brothers.
You play with your sisters and brothers.
I says, no, but I want to play with my friends that I always played.
He didn't have, I guess, what to say.
So he would say, don't play with her.
She's not a nice person, that she doesn't want to play with you.
Yes.
Well, the Hungarians came in 1938, '39.
And already, we start feeling there are new laws always came out against Jews.
They would-- the first thing they would take away, they took away the business.
That a Jew couldn't own any businesses.
So we had-- we had a grocery, like, a general store that my mother used to run it.
And we lost the license.
So-- Who ran the store after she died?
After she died, we lived with an aunt.
She took care on it.
So the youngest-- my mother's younger sister.
So she took care.
So we lost-- she-- we had-- we had cigarettes, and-- and a license for that.
So we lost that license.
We lost the license for the grocery.
And so we closed it up.
So that was the first thing.
First, the business was taken away.
So already, the people felt some people would take a non-Jewish, and they would run the business.
They got something for it.
But we just closed up.
Was--
And then, little by little, they always came with new-- new store-- new things.
Then they said, we can't travel from one city to another.
We had to have a permit-- the Jewish people had to have a permit to go from one city to the other.
And then they said, the Jewish kids can't go for higher education.
So that the boys and the girls that were going-- went to colleges-- I have-- I had a cousin that went to Munkacs to college, he came home.
And I remember, we went there, my aunt was crying, that he can't finish his education.
And he was such a brilliant boy.
He had a full scholarship to go to college.
And he was, like, in the family, we looked up to him.
He was like a star.
And he-- he didn't have any job.
And he stayed home.
He was so depressed.
My aunt was crying, that he's so depressed.
He came home.
So first the colleges, and then the high schools.
And then, to them-- I was in the fifth grade.
I couldn't go to elementary school either.
What years were these, that these laws were taking effect?
Well, my cousin, I think, in 1941.
Because it was just before the Jewish people were supposed to get Hungarian citizenship papers.
They came out that [INAUDIBLE] we had to have Hungarian citizenship papers.
So if you're not going to have Hungarian citizenship, they're going to deport us.
So how could we get the Hungarian citizenship papers, when we were Czechoslovakian?
But there-- there were people, like, in city hall.
And all they wanted was money.
So if you had enough money, you were able to buy yourself that you were a Hungarian citizen.
So I remember my father-- rest in peace-- he-- he was like-- knew the-- the guy in city hall.
And first he took $100, like $100, 100 pengo, then 150.
And there were a lot of people that couldn't come up with the money.
It was a lot of money to get citizenship papers.
And we were so scared because once they took away somebody from this town, we never knew what happened to the person.
They never returned.
So when my cousin, just before he came from college, home, he went to Munkacs, in college.
So I remember he says, well-- he was depressed-- so he says, maybe they're going to deport me.
But they took him then, to-- to-- not to the army.
It was like working.
You know, the Jewish boys went, like, to the army, but they took.
So he went away.
So my aunt says, he was so depressed, that he-- she was glad that he-- he left.
But then when it came that they don't know where he is, he disappeared, she was very upset, my aunt.
And most of the boys went to a labor camp.
So this was in 1940-- 40-- 41.
So a lot of people in my hometown, from my family, my mother's cousins, some-- my-- they couldn't come up with the money.
And they didn't have the Hungarian citizenship papers.
So they came one night.
I'll never forget.
And we heard screams and yelling.
And-- and they came, the Hungarian gendarmerie.
And they took the-- they lived there-- right across the street from us, my mother's cousins.
To-- and one, he was during the war, the First World War, my cousin-- my mother's cousin, he was an invalid.
He didn't have any legs.
So he got money from the Hungarian government for it, because he was an invalid.
And he couldn't come up with the money that he was-- he's a Hungarian citizen.
So they deported him.
So the screams, I'll never forget, how terrible those screams were.
So they took them away.
And we never know what happened to these people.
They took away about six families from our town.
And one person returned.
And he told us that they took him to Poland.
And they took the girls, some beautiful girls, that they gave them to the soldiers.
And the-- some people, they just threw in the river, in [NON-ENGLISH].
And they dug out ditches, and they shot the people.
And all were killed.
And he was, like, he had a nervous breakdown.
He was hiding in a different town from-- from us.
We would go to see him sometime-- my aunt would sometimes came.
It was a big secret that he came back.
Because if they would know, they would take him back.
And every time there was, at night, I was so scared to go to sleep.
Because I was afraid they're going to take us away.
This was in 1941, they took away these people.
I was home.
Yes.
I remember my father came home with the newspaper.
And he says that the war-- that they occupied-- they went in-- this, somehow, I remembered, that Germany went to Poland, is occupying Poland.
And they bombed.
And there are a lot of dead people there.
But it was, like, so far away from us, that it didn't affect me so-- so bad.
But when the Hungarians came in, before the Hungarians, the Czechs left.
And before the Hungarians came, there were a different government took over.
And this was-- they were called, [NON-ENGLISH].
And they-- they really said they're going to kill all the Jews-- openly, they said.
They're going to take over the Jewish property, and they're going to kill the Jews.
And who were the [NON-ENGLISH]?
These were boys from the town that we lived.
They grew up, they went-- were older, like, they went with my sisters to school.
Young boys, they joined this government.
So they divided-- they already said who's going to take our house.
So I knew this guy that said, I'm going to have your house.
And my father says, he's not going to live to see the-- he's not going to live that long, to have our house.
But he used to come on Saturday and watch this girl couldn't come in to make fire for us on Saturday.
And he would sit-- would sit on the steps-- and he-- he belonged to the [NON-ENGLISH]-- and he says-- her name was Anna-- Anna, if you step up those steps, I'm going to shoot you.
I have a gun.
And she says, you are crazy.
I'm not going to go and make fire for the kids.
They're cold.
Then milk the cow.
She says, you better don't step-- go on the steps.
He took out the gun.
And this girl was afraid, so she didn't come in.
But I remember, she spit in his face.
And it gave me satisfaction that she-- she did this.
And this-- but when the Hungarians came in, they were fighting the Hungarians.
And it was right in front of our house.
They were shooting.
And I remembered my father says, we should lay down, because the bullets were flying.
And I was so scared, you know?
And I says, oh, the war is here.
I felt that the war is not far away anymore.
The war is here.
So when the Hungarian came in, my father says, they a little bit better than the [NON-ENGLISH].
At least these are-- So these [NON-ENGLISH] were rounded up, and they-- they killed them.
The Hungarians killed these-- these young boys, a lot of them.
And the priest from our town also belonged to the [NON-ENGLISH], but he came home.
They let him come home.
So this was before the Hungarians occupied.
So we had, already, the peasants turned against the Jews.
So I felt that-- we felt it very strong, you know the anti-Semitism.
There were some people that they say-- I remembered this boy that came.
His name was Yoji.
I says-- you know, to the mother-- my aunt said, to the mother, how could you let your son be such a bad boy?
She says, ah, he's a child.
He wasn't child.
He was about 20, 21 years old.
He wasn't child anymore.
We didn't have much to do with the priest, really.
He-- we always knew that he's an anti-Semite.
Because he used to preach-- they used to tell us, some peasants used to tell us that he used to preach in church against the Jews, specially before Christmas.
So before Christmas, the-- some-- Jew-- some non-Jews, the Jews used to be afraid of the-- of the-- at Christmastime, of the peasants.
So you wouldn't-- what would happen on Christmas?
Well, I don't think anything happened.
But they used to say, that the priest had a sermon.
And he said that we killed their God, and they should not bother with us.
And they should, you know?
Some of them were nice-- they told us.
So the Jews stayed away from the priest.
But we had other people that we were very close, like the-- in the-- in the-- the principal from school.
He was very close with my father.
And he used to tell us things.
Because he used to go to church.
And he used to say that he discussions with the priest, that he shouldn't make the-- the-- the non-Jewish people so anti-Semite against the Jews.
But he says, he couldn't convince him.
The principal used to say, I can't convince him.
OK.
